## WORKFLOW COORDINATOR SYNTHESIS — RANKED ISSUES FOR EXEC REVIEW

Six issues from the 2026-05-19 report are now resolved. Two new P0/P1 issues were introduced by the TICKET-024 push notification implementation. One P2 issue (BUG-008) is only partially mitigated. The exec priority ranking is at the bottom.

## SECTION 2 — RESOLVED ITEMS (CONFIRMED)

### ✅ EX-001 — Exercise Library screen blank (RESOLVED)

`exercise-library.tsx` now correctly uses two distinct code paths:
- **Text search:** `GET /exercises/search?q=<query>` → `{ query, results: RawExercise[] }` — correctly reads `res.data.results`.
- **Browse-all / category filter:** `GET /exercises?kind=cardio` → `{ exercises: Record<string, RawExercise[]> }` — correctly flattens with `Object.values(grouped).flat()`.

Category chip → `kind` param mapping is present and correct. The screen is fully functional for all personas. **Not regressed.**

### ✅ PLANS-001 — `is_active` end-to-end wiring (RESOLVED)

All three required parts confirmed:
1. `GET /plans` and `GET /plans/:id` both include `is_active` in SELECT; list is sorted `is_active DESC`.
2. `POST /plans/:id/activate` opens a transaction, deactivates siblings first, activates target, releases client in `finally`.
3. `Plan` interface has `is_active: boolean`. `plans.tsx` `PlanCard` shows an ACTIVE badge with 2px accent border on the active plan and a "Set as active" button on all other user-owned cards. Single-flight guard via `activatingPlanId` state. **Not regressed.**

### ✅ P1-008 — AI Plan per-exercise coaching notes (RESOLVED)

`plans.js` system prompt HARD RULES (line 415): `"Every exercise MUST include a coaching_note: one concise sentence (10–20 words)..."` — present and mandatory. `coaching_note` in the JSON schema at line 427. `PlanExercise` interface in `api.ts` has `coaching_note?: string`. `ExerciseRow` in `plans.tsx` renders the note in italic `textTertiary` when present (lines 412–418). **Not regressed.**

### ✅ CSV-001 — Null-duration dedup (RESOLVED)

Replaced per-row SQL with a batch dedup approach. The dedup key is `"YYYY-MM-DD|<duration_seconds or NULL>"` — null-safe via `p.duration_seconds != null ? String(p.duration_seconds) : 'NULL'`. Strava rows with blank `Moving Time` are correctly deduplicated. **Not regressed.**

### ✅ CSV-002 — `activity_type` data loss (RESOLVED)

Migration `20260519_workouts_activity_type.sql` adds the column with CHECK constraint and a partial index. `csvImport.js` INSERT includes `activity_type` in both column list and values array (column 1 of 6 in the bulk unnest). **Not regressed.**

### ✅ POOL-001 — CSV per-row sequential queries (RESOLVED, ahead of schedule)

`csvImport.js` now uses a 3-phase approach: (1) date-range pre-filter, (2) single batch dedup SELECT, (3) single bulk INSERT wrapped in `BEGIN`/`COMMIT`/`ROLLBACK`. A mid-import failure now rolls back cleanly. Performance for a 200-row Strava export drops from ~400 sequential round-trips to 3. **Resolved one sprint ahead of schedule.**

---

## SECTION 3 — OPEN ISSUES (CARRY-FORWARD)

### ⚠️ BUG-008 — `confirmedThisSession` still in-memory only (PARTIALLY MITIGATED)

**File:** `mobile/app/(tabs)/rankings.tsx` (line 589)

The fix applied at line 709 adds `|| ranking.confirmed_1rm_kg != null` as a fallback, which correctly handles the post-batch case (confirmed + batch has run → `confirmed_1rm_kg` is non-null from the API → CTA stays hidden across restarts). **This resolves the most common restart scenario.**

However, the underlying state is still `useState<Set<string>>(() => new Set())` (line 589) — fully in-memory. The residual gap: a user confirms their 1RM, closes the app **before** the nightly batch runs, then reopens. At that point `confirmed_1rm_kg` is still null (batch hasn't run yet) and `confirmedThisSession` is empty (wiped by restart) → the "Confirm your max" CTA reappears.

**Impact:** Reduced from "always reappears on restart" to "reappears only if user restarts before the next cron batch." In practice this is a same-night edge case. Still worth a one-line fix (persist `confirmedThisSession` to AsyncStorage) to eliminate entirely, but no longer a launch blocker.

**Revised severity:** 🟢 P3 (downgraded from P2 given partial mitigation).

### ❌ P0-003 — HealthKit `requestHealthKitPermissions` is a stub (UNCHANGED)

**File:** `mobile/src/services/healthKit.ts` (lines 66–91)

`requestHealthKitPermissions()` still returns `false` immediately and logs `[HealthKit] stub: react-native-health not yet installed`. The `react-native-health` import is commented out. This blocks the HealthKit onboarding step and the health-metrics dashboard for all iOS users. Requires EAS build + Apple device to verify once unblocked. No change since prior reports.

---

## SECTION 4 — NEW ISSUES

### 🔴 PUSH-001 — Push token type mismatch: Expo relay token stored where FCM registration token is expected — **ALL PUSH NOTIFICATIONS WILL FAIL**

**Files:** `mobile/src/services/pushNotifications.ts` (line 58), `peak-fettle-agents/server/cron/push-dispatcher.js` (lines 43–76)

**Evidence:**

The mobile client registers for push notifications using:
```typescript
const token = await Notifications.getExpoPushTokenAsync();
await patchProfile({ fcm_token: token.data });
```

`getExpoPushTokenAsync()` returns an **Expo push token** in the format `"ExponentPushToken[xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx]"`. This token is a handle into Expo's push relay service (`https://exp.host/--/api/v2/push/send`) — it is **not** a raw FCM registration token.

The backend cron dispatcher sends notifications via the **FCM Legacy HTTP API** directly:
```js
const response = await fetch('https://fcm.googleapis.com/fcm/send', {
  method: 'POST',
  headers: { Authorization: `key=${FCM_SERVER_KEY}` },
  body: JSON.stringify({ to: fcmToken, ... })
});
```

FCM's `to` field expects a **raw FCM device registration token** (format: `"cXXXXXXXXXXX:APA91..."` — a long alphanumeric string). When an Expo push token is passed to FCM's `to` field, FCM returns an `InvalidRegistration` or `NotRegistered` error. The dispatcher catches this error and clears the token (`UPDATE users SET fcm_token = NULL`), meaning the token is deleted from the DB after the first dispatch attempt. All subsequent notifications are silently skipped because `fcm_token IS NULL`.

**Net effect:** Push notifications are fully non-functional for all users on all platforms. The token is stored, the dispatcher runs, FCM rejects it, the token is cleared. Zero notifications delivered.

**Two valid fixes (mutually exclusive):**

**Option A — Switch server to Expo Push API (recommended, lower effort):**
Replace the FCM Legacy API call in `push-dispatcher.js` with a call to `https://exp.host/--/api/v2/push/send`. Expo's relay accepts `ExponentPushToken[xxx]` format directly, handles Android/iOS routing internally, and does not require `FCM_SERVER_KEY`. The `to` field accepts the stored token as-is. Removes the dependency on `FCM_SERVER_KEY` env var. Effort: **S** — replace one `fetch()` call and its response-parsing block.

**Option B — Switch client to raw FCM token (higher effort):**
Replace `getExpoPushTokenAsync()` with `Notifications.getDevicePushTokenAsync()` on Android (returns raw FCM token) and `getExpoPushTokenAsync()` on iOS (APNs token; Expo relay needed). Requires `google-services.json` on Android (see PUSH-002). Effort: **M** — platform-conditional token retrieval + server must handle both token formats.

**Recommendation:** Option A. The Expo Push API is purpose-built for React Native / Expo apps, eliminates `FCM_SERVER_KEY` env dependency, and requires no mobile-side changes.

**Affected personas:** All — Marcus, Derek, Sofia, Priya. Push notifications are a confirmed retention feature (group streak alerts, workout reminders).

---

### 🟠 PUSH-002 — Android FCM: `google-services.json` not configured in `app.json` — Android push token registration will fail at build time

**File:** `mobile/app.json`

**Evidence:**

`app.json` includes `"expo-notifications"` in the plugins array but the Android block has no `googleServicesFile` entry:
```json
"android": {
  "adaptiveIcon": { ... },
  "package": "com.peakfettle.app"
  // ← NO "googleServicesFile": "./google-services.json"
}
```

Without this, `expo-notifications` cannot configure the Android FCM client during the EAS prebuild step. The EAS prebuild will either fail with `ENOENT google-services.json` or build without FCM, in which case `getExpoPushTokenAsync()` throws on Android devices.

Note: if PUSH-001 is resolved via **Option A** (Expo Push API), push notifications will still work on Android without `google-services.json` — Expo's own build infrastructure handles the FCM relay. However, if Option B is chosen (raw FCM token), both `google-services.json` presence and the `app.json` config entry are mandatory.

**Required fix (Option A path):** If resolving PUSH-001 via Option A, verify Expo's own Firebase project handles Android delivery. No `google-services.json` needed in the app repo — remove this blocker from the pre-launch list.

**Required fix (Option B path):** Add `"googleServicesFile": "./google-services.json"` to the `android` block in `app.json` and commit the `google-services.json` file (from Firebase Console → Project settings → Your apps → Android app → Download).

**Revised severity if Option A chosen for PUSH-001:** 🟢 P3 (no action needed). If Option B chosen: 🔴 P0.

## EXEC SUMMARY — RANKED PRIORITY LIST (WORKFLOW COORDINATOR)

**Rank 1 — PUSH-001 (🔴 P0 — fix before any push-dependent QA or launch)**
All push notifications are silently failing due to a token format mismatch. Expo push tokens cannot be sent directly to FCM's Legacy HTTP API. Option A fix (switch server to `https://exp.host/--/api/v2/push/send`) is an S-effort, server-only change that eliminates the issue without any mobile changes. This must be resolved before EAS build testing — otherwise every push test will appear to fail even after other infra is in order.

**Rank 2 — P0-003 HealthKit stub (🔴 P0 — pre-App Store submission)**
`requestHealthKitPermissions()` is a no-op stub. iOS users who grant HealthKit access on the onboarding screen get nothing. Requires `react-native-health` install + EAS build. Not a launch blocker if HealthKit is gated behind a feature flag, but must be resolved before App Store submission due to the `NSHealthShareUsageDescription` entitlement being present in `app.json`'s iOS config (Apple reviewers will check it works).

**Rank 3 — PUSH-002 (🟠 P1 or 🟢 P3 — depends on PUSH-001 resolution)**
If PUSH-001 is fixed via Option A (Expo Push API, recommended), PUSH-002 is a non-issue — Expo handles Android FCM internally. If PUSH-001 is fixed via Option B, PUSH-002 becomes a hard blocker for Android builds. Resolve Rank 1 first; this rank resolves itself.

**Rank 4 — BUG-008 `confirmedThisSession` (🟢 P3 — post-launch if needed)**
Downgraded from P2. The partial fix covers the common restart-after-batch scenario. Residual gap is a same-night edge case. A one-line AsyncStorage write eliminates it entirely but is not a launch blocker.

**Rank 5 — CSV-003 Strava pace unit (🟡 P2 — before Priya QA on CSV import)**
The `1000 / speed` formula is clamped (returns null for implausible values) so no corrupt data is written. Still needs a real Strava `activities.csv` to confirm whether the unit is m/s or km/h. A 60-second action from anyone with a Strava metric account unblocks it.

**Rank 6 — EAS Build (🔴 INFRA — highest-leverage infrastructure action)**
Unblocks all device testing, TICKET-025, TICKET-027, PUSH-001 verification, P0-003 (HealthKit), and App Store submission. All code-level issues above except PUSH-001 are already fixed in code — EAS Build is the only remaining gate.

**Rank 7 — Outstanding exec decisions (OD-5)**
Tab architecture (Progress vs. Log as Tab 2) remains the single largest decision blocking the Phase F screen layout freeze. No new UX feedback surfaced this pass.

The May-21 mega-commit (`63038c3`) closed **6 of 10** tracked issues in a single session (EX-001, PLANS-001, P1-008, CSV-001, CSV-002, POOL-001). POOL-001 was resolved a full sprint ahead of schedule. The codebase is in substantially better shape than the 2026-05-19 snapshot. The two new PUSH issues are implementation-layer wiring gaps, not architectural problems — both resolve with small, targeted changes.
